title: [Feature]: Add push-consumer capability and configuration guardrails
problem: Push-consumer support requires a separate set of NATS client capabilities and consumer settings. Without fail-closed validation, a future push mode could accidentally run with auto-ACK behavior, unsafe pending buffers, missing flow-control support, or ambiguous client-version behavior.
proposal: Add a push-consumer capability and configuration layer that validates the installed nats-py API, requires manual acknowledgement, validates deliver subject and optional deliver group values, bounds client pending message and byte limits, and makes FlowControl and IdleHeartbeat handling explicit. The layer should be separate from the production pull runner and should fail closed when required support is absent.
users: Maintainers implementing push-consumer support and operators who need predictable configuration validation before enabling an opt-in push mode.
delivery_semantics: Capability checks must protect the commit-then-ACK invariant by refusing any configuration that could ACK automatically or make in-flight work unbounded. Pull-mode behavior must remain unchanged.
security: Configuration errors must not print credentials, private URLs, sensitive subject families, or environment details. Deliver subjects, group names, and limits must be validated with allow lists and bounded ranges.
acceptance: Push capability detection uses documented public nats-py APIs where available.
Manual ACK is required for every push mode configuration.
Pending message and byte limits are bounded and validated.
FlowControl and IdleHeartbeat support is detected or the configuration fails closed.
Pull-mode configuration and runtime behavior are unchanged.
tests: Add unit tests with fake client/API objects for supported, unsupported, partial, and exception-raising capability states. Add configuration tests for unsafe pending limits, missing manual ACK, invalid deliver subjects, invalid deliver groups, and redacted errors. Run scripts/check.sh.
documentation: Update push-consumer evaluation, configuration, NATS feature gap, operations, testing, roadmap, and changelog documentation.
closeout: Close after the release containing push-consumer capability and configuration guardrails is published. Include release tag, tests, documentation, and limitations.
